overview.body: Look at the top of main() in your lab-5-1.py. You'll see ten state variables: current_city, total_miles, visited, unique_cities, unique_hazards, and five more. Every one of them is related — they all describe the same thing: the player's journey. This week, you'll bundle all ten into a single Player class. The Player object tracks everything, and the main program talks to it through method calls. lab-5-2.py is provided — your job is to build player.py so it runs.

mentorQuote.quote: You wrote the same game five different ways this semester. Each time it got better. Module 1 was raw Python. Module 3 added files. Module 4 added dictionaries. Module 5 brings it home with objects. That's not repetition -- that's how you learn to build things that last.

objectives: Define a Player class using the same OOP pattern as City
Track all game state in one object: location, miles, cities visited, hazards, and trivia
Use a list and a set inside a class to track the full route and unique cities
Replace ten scattered state variables with a single player object
See the provided lab-5-2.py as a spec: every player. call tells you a method to build
programRequirements: player.py defines a class named Player with private attributes
Player has twelve getter-style methods for all attributes, including get_unique_city_count() and get_unique_hazard_count() which return lengths rather than raw attributes
travel_to(city_name, distance) updates current city, miles, visited list, and unique set
record_hazard(hazard_name, escaped) updates hazard counters and unique hazard set
get_route_string() returns the full route as a ' -> ' separated string
get_trivia_accuracy() returns accuracy as a percentage string
__str__ method returns a single-line summary of the player
overview.filenameNote: Submit city.py (carried from 5.1), player.py (new this week), lab-5-2.py (provided -- do not modify), and function_library.py this week.
overview.additionalFilesNote: lab-5-2.py is provided in full on this page. Do not modify it. Read through it carefully -- every player. call tells you a method your Player class needs to implement.
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 10: class definition, __init__ constructor, private attributes with double-underscore prefix, getter methods, state-updating methods, __str__ special method, and list/set as class attributes.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 10

rows.task: Add display methods and __str__, run test.py

timeGuide.strategy: Start by reading through the provided lab-5-2.py and noting every player. call. That list is your build checklist. The Player class follows the exact same pattern as the City class you built in Lab 5.1 -- private attributes, getters, methods that update state, and __str__. If City clicked, Player will too.

beforeYouBegin.intro: The Player class replaces ten scattered variables from lab-5-1.py's main() function. Each attribute maps directly to one of those variables. Each method maps to the lines that updated them. Use this architecture as your build guide.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- player.py ----
class Player:

    # --- Constructor ---   [CP1]
    __init__(self, name, starting_city)
        self.__name              = name
        self.__current_city      = starting_city
        self.__total_miles       = 0
        self.__visited_cities    = [starting_city]   (list -- preserves order)
        self.__unique_cities     = set()             (set -- no duplicates)
        self.__unique_cities.add(starting_city)
        self.__hazards_faced     = 0
        self.__hazards_escaped   = 0
        self.__unique_hazards    = set()
        self.__trivia_asked      = 0
        self.__trivia_correct    = 0

    # --- Getter Methods ---   [CP2]
    get_name()                  returns self.__name
    get_current_city()          returns self.__current_city
    get_total_miles()           returns self.__total_miles
    get_visited_cities()        returns self.__visited_cities.copy()
    get_unique_cities()         returns self.__unique_cities.copy()
    get_unique_city_count()     returns len(self.__unique_cities)
    get_hazards_faced()         returns self.__hazards_faced
    get_hazards_escaped()       returns self.__hazards_escaped
    get_unique_hazards()        returns self.__unique_hazards.copy()
    get_unique_hazard_count()   returns len(self.__unique_hazards)
    get_trivia_asked()          returns self.__trivia_asked
    get_trivia_correct()        returns self.__trivia_correct

    # --- Travel Methods ---   [CP3]
    travel_to(city_name, distance)
        updates: __current_city, __total_miles, __visited_cities, __unique_cities

    # --- Hazard Methods ---   [CP3]
    record_hazard(hazard_name, escaped)
        updates: __hazards_faced, __unique_hazards
        if escaped: updates __hazards_escaped

    # --- Trivia Methods ---   [CP3]
    record_trivia(correct)
        updates: __trivia_asked
        if correct: updates __trivia_correct

    # --- Display Methods ---   [CP4]
    get_route_string()          ' -> '.join(self.__visited_cities)
    get_trivia_accuracy()       returns percentage string e.g. '75.0%'
    __str__                     returns 'Maria in Houston (450 miles)'


# ---- lab-5-2.py ---- (PROVIDED -- do not modify)
from city import City
from player import Player
import function_library

  ... all load functions unchanged ...
  build_cities()        same as lab-5-1.py
  trivia_gate()         same as lab-5-1.py
  handle_hazard(hazards, player)     [REPLACED] calls player.record_hazard()
  execute_travel(dest, dist, ...)    [REPLACED] calls player.travel_to()

main():
  player = Player(name, 'San Antonio')   [NEW] one line replaces ten variables
  ... game loop calls player methods ...

if __name__ == '__main__': -> main()

checkpoints.title: File Setup & Constructor
checkpoints.subtitle: Create player.py and define the Player class with all ten private attributes.

content.body: The Player class starts with one job: hold all the state that was scattered across main() in lab-5-1.py. Ten variables become ten private attributes, all initialized in one __init__ method.

In lab-5-1.py, if you wanted to know the player's current city, you'd look at current_city. If you wanted their total miles, total_miles. Two different names, two different places. Now it's player.get_current_city() and player.get_total_miles(). Same object. Same place. Everything the player is lives in one place.

references.description: You built a CellPhone class with private attributes for manufacturer, model, and price. Player follows the same pattern -- more attributes, same structure. Copy your City class to player.py, rename the class, and replace the attributes using the architecture diagram above.

content.quote: When I first saw ten state variables in main() I thought that was just how games worked. Then Danny showed me a Player class and I literally said 'that's it?' One object. One place. Everything the player is, right there. I wish I'd learned this sooner.

items: Create a new file called player.py
Add the module docstring at the top with your name, abc123, section, and description
Define the Player class with a class docstring
Write __init__(self, name, starting_city) and initialize all ten private attributes -- use the architecture diagram as your guide
Pay attention to the list and set: self.__visited_cities = [starting_city] starts with the starting city already in it, and so does self.__unique_cities

content.body: Step 1: Add this test at the bottom of player.py:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
p = Player('Maria', 'San Antonio')
print('Player created successfully!')
print(type(p))

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Inheriting from City: class Player(City):
Player does not inherit from City -- they are separate classes. Inheritance is Chapter 11 and not part of this course.
Not pre-loading starting_city into the list and set
The starting city counts as visited from the moment the game begins
Using a single underscore: self._name
Private attributes need double underscores: self.__name

items: player.py with module docstring
Player class with class docstring
__init__ accepting name and starting_city
All ten private attributes initialized
Starting city pre-loaded in both __visited_cities and __unique_cities

content.body: Twelve getter methods that provide read access to the Player's private data. These are the methods lab-5-2.py calls to build the final report. Same pattern you used in City -- each getter just returns its attribute.

Getters enforce a contract: outside code never touches private data directly. lab-5-2.py doesn't reach into player.__total_miles — it calls player.get_total_miles(). That one layer of separation is what makes it safe to change how the Player stores data internally without breaking anything that uses it. Two of the getters — get_unique_city_count() and get_unique_hazard_count() — return len() instead of the raw attribute. The caller gets a number, not a set. That is encapsulation doing real work.

references.description: You typed get_manufact(), get_model(), and get_retail_price() -- each just returned self.__attribute. All twelve Player getters follow exactly the same pattern.

content.quote: Twelve getters sounds like a lot of repetition. But think of it as a public API. lab-5-2.py is the client. It only talks to Player through method calls. If we ever need to change how the Player stores miles internally, the client code doesn't change at all. That contract is worth writing twelve methods for.

items: Add a # --- Getter Methods --- comment below __init__

Write a getter for each of the twelve methods listed in the architecture diagram -- ten return self.__attribute directly, and two (get_unique_city_count() and get_unique_hazard_count()) return len(self.__attribute)
For get_visited_cities() and get_unique_cities(), return a copy with .copy() to protect the originals
Add a docstring to every method

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
p = Player('Maria', 'San Antonio')
print(f'Name: {p.get_name()}')
print(f'City: {p.get_current_city()}')
print(f'Miles: {p.get_total_miles()}')
print(f'Unique cities: {p.get_unique_city_count()}')
print(f'Hazards faced: {p.get_hazards_faced()}')
print(f'Trivia asked: {p.get_trivia_asked()}')

content.output:
Name: Maria
City: San Antonio
Miles: 0
Unique cities: 1
Hazards faced: 0
Trivia asked: 0

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Returning the raw set instead of a copy: return self.__unique_cities
The caller could modify the set directly. Use return self.__unique_cities.copy() to protect the original.
Returning self.__unique_cities for the count getter
get_unique_city_count() returns len(self.__unique_cities), not the set itself.
Missing a docstring on any getter
CodeGrade checks documentation on every method. One missing docstring is a deduction.

items: Twelve getter methods, each with a docstring
get_visited_cities() and get_unique_cities() return copies

checkpoints.title: State-Updating Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add travel_to(), record_hazard(), and record_trivia() -- the methods that update the player's state during the game.

content.body: Three methods that replace the scattered update statements in lab-5-1.py's main() function. Each method handles multiple related updates in one place. Instead of four lines of code to record travel, it's one method call: player.travel_to(dest, distance).

In lab-5-1.py, recording a hazard meant four separate lines in main(): increment the counter, add to the set, check a boolean, maybe increment escaped. Now it is one call: player.record_hazard(name, escaped). The Player knows how to update itself. The caller just says what happened. This is what object-oriented design means in practice — behavior lives with the data it operates on.

content.quote: Three methods. travel_to(), record_hazard(), record_trivia(). Together they replace about twenty lines of scattered updates in main(). And next time we want to add a stat — say, a fuel tracker — we add one attribute and one method. We don't go hunting through main() trying to remember every place it touches.

references.description: The CellPhone class had a set_retail_price() method that updated self.__retail_price. Your travel_to(), record_hazard(), and record_trivia() methods follow the exact same pattern — a method that changes one or more private attributes based on what happened.
The BankAccount deposit() method did self.__balance = self.__balance + amount. Your travel_to() does the same thing with miles: self.__total_miles = self.__total_miles + distance.

items: Add a # --- Travel Methods --- comment
Define travel_to(self, city_name, distance) -- it must update all four travel attributes:

content.code:
def travel_to(self, city_name, distance):
    """
    Travel to a new city.

    Replaces in main():
        current_city = final_dest
        total_miles = total_miles + miles
        visited.append(final_dest)
        unique_cities.add(final_dest)
    """
    self.__current_city = city_name
    self.__total_miles = self.__total_miles + distance
    self.__visited_cities.append(city_name)
    self.__unique_cities.add(city_name)

items: Add a # --- Hazard Methods --- comment
Define record_hazard(self, hazard_name, escaped) -- update __hazards_faced, add to __unique_hazards, and increment __hazards_escaped only if escaped is True
Add a # --- Trivia Methods --- comment
Define record_trivia(self, correct) -- increment __trivia_asked always, increment __trivia_correct only if correct is True

content.body: Run python test.py from your project folder. All Player tests through the state-updating section should pass.

content.output:
# After player = Player('Maria', 'San Antonio')
# then player.travel_to('Houston', 200)
# then player.record_hazard("Speed trap!", False)
# then player.record_trivia(True)

print(player.get_current_city())   # Houston
print(player.get_total_miles())    # 200
print(player.get_unique_city_count())  # 2
print(player.get_hazards_faced())  # 1
print(player.get_hazards_escaped())  # 0
print(player.get_trivia_asked())   # 1
print(player.get_trivia_correct()) # 1

rows: Forgetting to update all four attributes in travel_to()
Missing any one means the final report will be wrong
Using = instead of += equivalent for miles
Write it out: self.__total_miles = self.__total_miles + distance
Always incrementing __hazards_escaped
Only increment if escaped is True -- use if escaped:

items: travel_to() updating four attributes, with docstring
record_hazard() updating faced count, unique set, and conditionally escaped count, with docstring
record_trivia() updating asked count and conditionally correct count, with docstring

checkpoints.title: Display Methods, __str__, and Final Test
checkpoints.subtitle: Add the display methods, __str__, run the full test suite, then submit.

content.body: The final methods: get_route_string() formats the travel history as a readable route, get_trivia_accuracy() calculates the accuracy percentage, and __str__ gives a one-line summary of the player's current state.

Display methods are the Player's voice. get_route_string() and get_trivia_accuracy() transform raw stored data into something readable — the same work a report generator or dashboard does professionally. __str__ is Python's protocol for making objects printable: implement it, and print(player) just works. These three methods complete the Player's public interface.

references.description: You typed a __str__ method that returned a formatted string. Python calls it automatically when you print() the object. Your Player's __str__ should return something like 'Maria in Houston (450 miles)'.

content.quote: Fifty-three tests. That's what test.py runs against your Player class. Every method, every edge case, zero-trivia-asked, escaped vs. not escaped. Run it after every checkpoint — not just at the end. A test that breaks at CP2 is a five-minute fix. The same break discovered at submission is a bad night.

items: Add a # --- Display Methods --- comment
Define get_route_string(self) that joins the visited cities list with ' -> ':

Define get_trivia_accuracy(self) that returns a percentage string. If no questions have been asked, return '0.0%'. Otherwise calculate: (correct / asked) * 100 and return it formatted to one decimal place with a % sign.
Define __str__(self) that returns a single-line summary including the player name, current city, and total miles
Run python test.py -- all 53 tests should pass
Then run python lab-5-2.py and play the game end-to-end -- this is the payoff

content.body: When your Player class is complete, the final report in lab-5-2.py will look like this:

content.output:
=======================================================
FINAL TRIP REPORT
=======================================================
Driver:              Maria
Final City:          Corpus Christi

ROUTE:
  San Antonio -> Houston -> Corpus Christi

UNIQUE CITIES (3):
  - Corpus Christi
  - Houston
  - San Antonio

TRIVIA:
  Asked: 2
  Correct: 2
  Accuracy: 100.0%

HAZARDS:
  Faced: 1
  Escaped: 1
  Unique types: 1

STATS:
  Total Miles: 410

items: Run python test.py from your project folder. All 53 tests should pass. If any fail, the output tells you the method name and what it returned vs. what was expected.
Run python lab-5-2.py and play a complete game. Travel to at least two cities, trigger at least one hazard, and reach a beach or win city.
Verify print(player) inside the game loop outputs a clean one-line summary.
Verify the final report shows a correct route string with arrows, accurate trivia percentage, and correct unique city and hazard counts.
Check get_trivia_accuracy() with zero questions asked — quit before any trivia fires and confirm the report prints 0.0% without crashing.

rows: Returning self.__visited_cities directly in get_route_string()
' -> '.join() needs the list, not the return value of a method. Write it as ' -> '.join(self.__visited_cities) inside the method itself.
Dividing before checking if __trivia_asked is zero
If no trivia has been asked, __trivia_asked is 0. Guard with if self.__trivia_asked == 0: return '0.0%' before calculating.
Forgetting the % sign in the accuracy string
The return value should be a string like '75.0%' — include the percent sign.

items: get_route_string() joining visited cities with ' -> ', with docstring
get_trivia_accuracy() returning percentage string, handling zero-question case, with docstring
__str__ returning one-line player summary, with docstring
All 53 tests passing in test.py
Game runs end-to-end with lab-5-2.py
All four files submitted to CodeGrade

record_hazard() updates faced count, unique set, and conditionally escaped
record_trivia() updates asked count and conditionally correct
get_trivia_accuracy() handles the zero-question case without crashing

finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 only. Classes, constructors, private attributes, getter methods, and methods that update state. No inheritance -- class Player(City): is wrong and will be flagged by CodeGrade.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2-10 (all prior techniques fully permitted; Chapter 10 sections 10.1-10.3 introduced this module)
specific: Methods that update multiple private attributes
List (self.__visited_cities) and set (self.__unique_cities) as class attributes
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Inheritance, subclasses, method overriding, polymorphism (Chapter 11 -- not taught in this course)

warnings: Do not use inheritance. class Player(City): is a Chapter 11 technique and will be flagged as out-of-scope.
Do not modify lab-5-2.py. It is provided. CodeGrade will test your Player class against it.
Submit all four files. Missing any one will fail the CodeGrade import tests.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: This is the final lab of the semester. Keep everything -- city.py, player.py, lab-5-2.py, and function_library.py -- for your portfolio. You built a full game from scratch across five modules. That's worth keeping.

ifYouGetStuck: Read through lab-5-2.py and find every player. call -- each one is a method you need to write
Run python test.py -- it shows exactly which method is failing and what value it returned
The Player class is the City class with different attributes -- if City worked, Player will too
Check that class Player: does not inherit from City -- no parentheses with City inside
Come to Zoom or Discord -- this is the final lab and we want you to finish strong
